sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=57, col=23).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 129, level 3.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 74 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 2.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Excellent! Clicking preview box 2 DID change gameplay! 
Changes:
- Left H: gwwwggwwwggwwwggwwwg (all ON now, was OFF)
- Left V: ggBggggBggggBggggBgg (all OFF now, was ON)
- Right H: unchanged (ON)
- Right V: unchanged (ON)
- Preview box 2 is now selected (blue border)
So clicking preview boxes changes the LEFT button states! Each preview box corresponds to a different left button configuration.
Now I see:
- Preview box 1: Left H OFF, Left V ON
- Preview box 2: Left H ON, Left V OFF
Let me check preview boxes 3 and 4 to see their configurations. Then I need to figure out what the goal is.
Looking at the right panel, it has a checkerboard pattern with some blocks different colors. The right H and V buttons control which blocks are shown/hidden or colored differently.
Wait - maybe the right H and V buttons control the right panel's checkerboard pattern. And the goal is to make the right panel match some target configuration.
Let me check: the right panel has 4 rows and 6 columns of 4x4 blocks. The right H buttons (4 of them) control rows and right V buttons (6 of them) control columns.
Looking at the right panel:
- Row 1: black, black, magenta, magenta, black, black (with yellow L-shape on right)
- Row 2: black, black, magenta, magenta, black, black
- Row 3: black, black, black, black, black, black (with yellow square on left)
- Row 4: black, black, black, black, black, black
Actually, looking more carefully at the ascii, the right panel has:
- Black and charcoal blocks in a checkerboard pattern
- Magenta bar (vertical, 2 segments)
- Yellow objects
The right H/V buttons might control which blocks are "active" or "visible".
Let me try: click on the right H/V buttons to change the right panel configuration, then see if the level completes.
But first, let me check what preview boxes 3 and 4 do.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what preview box 3 does
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 36}])
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Preview 3: Left H={lines[33][6:26]}, Left V={lines[47][6:26]}")

# Check what preview box 4 does
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 49}])
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Preview 4: Left H={lines[33][6:26]}, Left V={lines[47][6:26]}")

# Go back to preview 1
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 10}])
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Preview 1: Left H={lines[33][6:26]}, Left V={lines[47][6:26]}")

# Now let me try to understand the right panel better
# Check right H and V button states
print(f"Right H: {lines[33][37:57]}")
print(f"Right V: {lines[47][37:57]}")

# Try turning OFF right V buttons and see what happens to right panel
# Right V buttons at cols 34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 59 (row 47)
# Currently: WWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWW - pattern is W,W,w,W,W,W,W,w,W,W,W,W,w,W,W,W,W,w,W,W
# So buttons 2, 7, 12 are ON (w), others OFF (W)

# Let me try turning ON all right V buttons
for col in [34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 59]:
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 47, 'col': col}])

lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nAfter toggling right V: {lines[47][37:57]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Preview 3: Left H=gwwwggwwwggwwwggwwwg, Left V=ggwggggwggggwggggwgg
Preview 4: Left H=gwwwggwwwggwwwggwwwg, Left V=ggwggggwggggwggggwgg
Preview 1: Left H=gwwwggwwwggwwwggwwwg, Left V=ggwggggwggggwggggwgg
Right H: WwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwW
Right V: WWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWW

After toggling right V: WWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWW
